Thank you for your letter of November 25, 1975, reques::-
ing an opinion with respect to your obligations under the 
Political Reform Act. 
Since your request does not raise a 
novel question involving interprEta~ion of the Act, no formal 
opinion will be issued. 
I hope, however, that the following 
comments "viII be helpful. 
It is the informal view of the staff that your gifts of 
smoked turkeys need not be reported by you so long as you eng2ge 
in no other activities \'lhich constitut_e attempts to influenc.::: 
state legislative or administrative action. 
Government Code 
Section 86108(b) generally requires that a person r~ust report 
all payments made for the purpose of influencing state legislative 
or administrative action if such pc:.:/ments exceed two hundred fifty 
dollars in a month. 
However, this reporting requirement does not 
apply if all such payments directly or indirectly benefit elective 
state off~cials, legislative officials, agency officials or cam-
bers of tne immediate family of any such official as described in 
GO'f:~rnment Code Section 82045 (c). 
The facts set forth in your 
l~~'~r indicate that all payments made by you which may constitute 
pa}-,::.::nts to influence state legislc:.tivc or administrative action 
wer~ made in the form of smoked turkeys given to such officials. 
On the basis of these facts, you nay continue to make gifts of 
smoked turkeys to these officials ~ithout incurring any reporting 
obligations under the Political Reform Act.

you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. AC2. Code Section 18321, a copy

For many years, Nicholas Turkey Bre2c::~3 Farms has shown its 
appreciation to its friends by presenting s~;-:'Jked turkeys during the ho!;day 
season. The fjrst recipients were on the s~aff at the Bank of Amsrica. 
They were selected because that instirJjO:1 has supported, for over forty 
years, what we have been trying to cchi2'.'e. Since thct beginning, more 
and more cherished relationships have CC:TIe into being, so \.\.-'e have 
given more and more turkeys. In 1973 v:e said "thank you" \.vith 
ninety-three smoked turkeys. 
1974 has been a disastrous ).:ear for the turkey industry. The spread 
between cost of production and market price received has varied bet\.\.'een 
three and fourteen cents a pound. Some companies in the U.S. raised 
over 200 million pounds this 1'ear. vVe m:::de a profit. Not as much as we 
had projected, but low enough so that \'~·2 are eliminating unnecessary 
costs. Our smoked turkey program is no~ being eliminated

you appreciate support the most? 
In 1974 we are giving one of these turk2)'s for each million turkeys 
produced in the U.S. There were 133 rT;:Ilion turkeys produced, so we are 
presenting 133 of these turkeys-Hone ;:1 a miUion." 
We have 27 pure and synthetic blood Hnes of turkeys on our farms. 
Five of these lines produce the genetic material we sell. Twenty lines will 
be maintained to try to improve what 1..':2 sell. Two of the present lines

sections.question: May we continue to give these turkeys? If so, what reporting requirements need we and the recipients follow?
sections.conclusion: It is the informal view of the staff that your gifts of smoked turkeys need not be reported by you so long as you engage in no other activities which constitute attempts to influence state legislative or administrative action. On the basis of these facts, you may continue to make gifts of smoked turkeys to these officials without incurring any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a turkey breeding company can gift smoked turkeys to state legislative and administrative officials and what reporting requirements apply under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that gifts of smoked turkeys to state officials do not require reporting under Government Code Section 86108(b) because the gifts directly benefit elective state officials and legislative officials as excepted under Section 82045(c).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal staff opinion issued in letter form rather than a formal opinion. The incoming request letter and company background materials are included with the FPPC response. No formal opinion was issued because the request did not raise a novel interpretation question.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May we continue to give these turkeys? If so, what reporting requirements need we and the recipients follow?

CONCLUSION: It is the informal view of the staff that your gifts of smoked turkeys need not be reported by you so long as you engage in no other activities which constitute attempts to influence state legislative or administrative action. On the basis of these facts, you may continue to make gifts of smoked turkeys to these officials without incurring any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC Response No. 75-210 to Nicholas Turkey Breeding Farms regarding whether gifts of smoked turkeys to state legislators and agency officials require reporting. Staff concluded no reporting is required as the gifts fall within the exemption for payments that directly benefit state officials.
